3. Answering with proverbs or idioms is also prohibited.

Private Thought of Tanaka: Okay, well, that was an unexpected interjection. Just when Ito-san and I were really hitting our stride, perfectly synced on this amazing concept, Yamamoto-kun pops in. He’s asking about how I’m feeling after "our initial brainstorming session" and wanting to hear *whatever's brewing*, no matter how unconventional. He even mentioned Sato-san's diverse ideas, which makes me think he's still in the "gathering all the raw thoughts" phase.

My mind was so focused on just diving deep with Ito-san, feeling like we had something truly special and didn't need any other input *right now*. But Yamamoto-kun is the leader, and he's specifically asking *me* to share my thoughts openly. It’s not that my idea with Ito-san isn't unconventional – it absolutely is, and it's brilliant! – but I was about to go into intricate details about narrative arcs and puzzle mechanics, and I don't know if he's ready for that level of specificity yet, or if he's just looking for a general direction from everyone.

I need to respond to him, of course. I can’t just say, "I'm good, I'm just going to keep working with Ito-san." That would be… not ideal. I should probably tell him about the concept I've been developing, or at least the core idea of it, and highlight its unique angle. I'll need to make sure I present it in a way that shows I've been actively thinking and collaborating, but also open enough to show I'm still part of the broader departmental brainstorming.

Maybe I can frame it as one of the strong avenues I've been exploring, rather than a fully locked-down plan, even though in my head, it absolutely feels like *the* plan. It's a good idea, definitely fits the "unconventional" brief. I just hope he sees the potential as much as Ito-san and I do.

So, who to talk to next? Well, I *have* to talk to Yamamoto-kun now, obviously. I need to formulate my thoughts on how to present this "digital archaeologist" idea in a way that satisfies his request for initial thoughts, while still conveying the excitement and depth Ito-san and I have found. After that, I’ll probably circle back to Ito-san to let him know I’ve presented our concept to Yamamoto-kun and see what he thinks our next steps should be based on the leader's feedback. I want to keep that momentum going with Ito-san, but I also need to navigate the departmental leader's expectations. (formed immediately after interacting with Yamamoto)
